papers.title: Activity and Energy Expenditure in Older People Playing Active Video Games Lynne M. Taylor, MSc, Ralph Maddison, PhD, Leila A. Pfaefﬂi, MA, Jonathan C. Rawstorn, BSc, Nicholas Gant, PhD, Ngaire M. Kerse, PhD, MBChB
Research Report The effects of an action video game on visual and affective

Editor’s key points Painful medical procedures are often unavoidable in paediatric medical care, which can cause signiﬁcant pain, anxiety or distress, and can lead to negative long-term outcomes. Screen-based technologies can improve procedural

studies.paper_title: Activity and Energy Expenditure in Older People Playing Active Video Games Lynne M. Taylor, MSc, Ralph Maddison, PhD, Leila A. Pfaefﬂi, MA, Jonathan C. Rawstorn, BSc, Nicholas Gant, PhD, Ngaire M. Kerse, PhD, MBChB
studies.study_label: Cross-sectional lab study of energy expenditure during active video game play
studies.context: University research center; laboratory-based testing (playing area 4 x 4 m, 32-inch flat screen); Nintendo Wii and Xbox 360 Kinect platforms used.
studies.intervention_duration: Each game played for 5 minutes; 5-minute seated rest interleaved between games; 20-minute familiarization; 10-minute supine rest for resting VO2.

interventions.intervention_name: Active video game play vs rest
interventions.treatment_raw: Participants played 9 active video games, each for 5 minutes, in random order. Two games (boxing and bowling) were played in both seated and standing positions.
interventions.control_raw: resting oxygen consumption per unit time (VˈO2) was measured while participants rested quietly, positioned supine for 10 minutes. Games were interleaved with 5 minutes of seated rest.

conditions.description: Play of active video games (each for 5 minutes) on Nintendo Wii and Xbox 360 Kinect in randomized order; included standing and seated play for some games.
Rest (supine rest to measure resting VO2; interleaved 5-minute seated rest between game trials).

outcomes.outcome_raw: energy expenditure (METs)
outcomes.measurement_raw: indirect calorimetry; energy expenditure expressed in kilojoules per minute and metabolic equivalents (METs); VˈO2 measured via expired air gas analysis system
outcomes.source_quote: Mean ± SD energy expenditure was signiﬁcantly greater for all game conditions compared with rest (all P≤.01) and ranged from 1.46±.41 METs to 2.97±1.16 METs.

outcomes.outcome_raw: difference in energy expenditure, activity counts, and perceived exertion between equivalent games played while standing and seated
outcomes.outcome_canonical: no significant difference between seated and standing play (energy expenditure, activity counts, perceived exertion)
outcomes.measurement_raw: indirect calorimetry; Actigraph accelerometers (hip and wrist) for activity counts; Ratings of Perceived Exertion (Borg scale) for perceived exertion
outcomes.source_quote: There was no signiﬁcant difference in energy expenditure, activity counts, or perceived exertion between equivalent games played while standing and seated.

outcomes.outcome_raw: correlation between energy expenditure or activity counts and balance status
outcomes.outcome_canonical: no significant correlation between energy expenditure or activity counts and balance status
outcomes.measurement_raw: balance assessed using the mini-BESTest, the Activities-speciﬁc Balance Conﬁdence (ABC) Scale, and the Timed Up and Go (TUG); correlations with EE and activity counts assessed
outcomes.source_quote: No signiﬁcant correlations were observed between energy expenditure or activity counts and balance status.

outcomes.outcome_raw: activity counts (accelerometry)
outcomes.measurement_raw: two dual-axial accelerometers (Actigraph Model ActiGraph GT1M) worn on the right hip and dominant wrist
outcomes.source_quote: To quantify movement, participants were instrumented with 2 dual-axial accelerometers (Actigraph Model ActiGraph GT1M) worn on the right hip and dominant wrist.

outcomes.outcome_raw: perceived exertion (RPE)
outcomes.measurement_raw: Ratings of Perceived Exertion (RPEs) Scale (Borg scale, range 6-20)
outcomes.source_quote: We measured self-perceived exercise intensity using the Ratings of Perceived Exertion (RPEs) Scale. The scale ranges from 6 (no exertion at all) to 20 (maximal exertion).

outcomes.outcome_raw: interpretation: active video games provide light-intensity exercise in community-dwelling older people
outcomes.source_quote: Active video games provide light-intensity exercise in community-dwelling older people, whether played while seated or standing.

outcomes.outcome_raw: interpretation: seated active video game play may provide equivalent benefits to standing play for people unable to stand
outcomes.outcome_canonical: equivalence of seated and standing play (interpretation)
outcomes.source_quote: People who are unable to stand may derive equivalent beneﬁts from active video games played while seated.

studies.paper_title: Research Report The effects of an action video game on visual and affective
studies.study_label: 10 h training: action video game vs non-action video game vs no-contact control
studies.context: event-related brain potentials (ERPs); emotion search task

interventions.treatment_raw: training non-gamers on an action ... video game for 10 h
training non-gamers on an ... non-action video game for 10 h

outcomes.outcome_raw: increase in the amplitude of the ERPs following training over the right frontal and posterior regions
outcomes.outcome_canonical: ERP amplitude increase (right frontal and posterior regions)
outcomes.measurement_raw: event-related brain potentials (ERPs); amplitude of the ERPs
outcomes.source_quote: Playing an action game resulted in two effects: one that reflected an increase in the amplitude of the ERPs following training over the right frontal and posterior regions that was similar for angry, happy, and neutral faces;

outcomes.outcome_raw: reduction in the allocation of attention to happy faces
outcomes.outcome_canonical: allocation of attention to happy faces (reduction)
outcomes.measurement_raw: inferred from ERP effects related to attention (P3) during emotion search task
outcomes.source_quote: Playing an action game resulted in ... one that reflected a reduction in the allocation of attention to happy faces.

outcomes.outcome_raw: changes in slow wave activity over the central–parietal and frontal regions that were greater for targets (i.e., angry and happy faces) than for nontargets (i.e., neutral faces)
outcomes.outcome_canonical: slow wave activity increase for targets (central–parietal and frontal regions)
outcomes.measurement_raw: slow wave activity (ERP slow wave) over central–parietal and frontal regions
outcomes.source_quote: In contrast, playing a non-action game resulted in changes in slow wave activity over the central–parietal and frontal regions that were greater for targets (i.e., angry and happy faces) than for nontargets (i.e., neutral faces).

outcomes.outcome_raw: N2 and P3 amplitudes are greater for targets than non-targets (general ERP background cited)
outcomes.outcome_canonical: N2 and P3 target > non-target amplitude
outcomes.measurement_raw: N2 (180–300 ms), P3 (300–600 ms) amplitude measured by ERPs
outcomes.source_quote: Studies examining the neural correlates of target processing in visual search tasks using ERPs have consistently demonstrated that the amplitude of the N2 and P3 components are greater for targets than non-targets (Bledowski et al., 2004; Luck and Hillyard, 1990, 1994).

studies.paper_title: Editor’s key points Painful medical procedures are often unavoidable in paediatric medical care, which can cause signiﬁcant pain, anxiety or distress, and can lead to negative long-term outcomes. Screen-based technologies can improve procedural
studies.study_label: Systematic review and meta-analysis of interactive video games vs standard care for paediatric procedural pain and anxiety
studies.context: We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of the effectiveness of interactive video games compared with standard care in children (0e18 yr) undergoing painful procedures.

interventions.intervention_name: Interactive video games vs standard care / non-interactive games
interventions.control_raw: standard care or other non-interactive video game interventions

conditions.description: For the purposes of this study, we have included all screen-based games with an audiovisual component that responds to user input to change the audiovisual output, and henceforth referred to these as 'interactive video games'.
standard care or other non-interactive video game interventions

outcomes.outcome_raw: children’s procedural pain
outcomes.measurement_raw: standardised mean difference (SMD)
outcomes.effect_size_raw: SMD¼e0.43; 95% conﬁdence interval (CI): e0.67 to e0.20
outcomes.source_quote: interactive video games appear to reduce children’s procedural pain (standardised mean difference [SMD]¼e0.43; 95% conﬁdence interval [CI]: e0.67 to e0.20)

outcomes.outcome_raw: children’s procedural anxiety
outcomes.measurement_raw: standardised mean difference (SMD)
outcomes.source_quote: interactive video games appear to reduce children’s ... anxiety (SMD¼0.61; 95% CI: e0.88 to e0.34)

outcomes.outcome_raw: caregivers’ procedural anxiety
outcomes.measurement_raw: standardised mean difference (SMD)
outcomes.source_quote: interactive video games appear to reduce children’s procedural pain ... and caregivers’ procedural anxiety (SMD¼e0.31; 95% CI: e0.58 to e0.04).

outcomes.outcome_raw: procedure length
outcomes.measurement_raw: meta-analysis comparison vs standard care
outcomes.effect_size_raw: no difference between interactive video games compared with standard care for most medical outcomes (e.g. procedure length)
outcomes.source_quote: We also observed no difference between interactive video games compared with standard care for most medical outcomes (e.g. procedure length), except a reduced need for restraint.

outcomes.outcome_raw: need for restraint
outcomes.measurement_raw: meta-analysis comparison vs standard care
outcomes.source_quote: We also observed no difference between interactive video games compared with standard care for most medical outcomes (e.g. procedure length), except a reduced need for restraint.

outcomes.outcome_raw: difference between preparatory and distracting games
outcomes.measurement_raw: meta-analysis subgroup comparison
outcomes.effect_size_raw: no difference between preparatory and distracting games
outcomes.source_quote: We observed no difference between preparatory and distracting games, or between virtual reality and non-virtual reality games.

outcomes.outcome_raw: virtual reality vs non-virtual reality games
outcomes.measurement_raw: meta-analysis subgroup comparison
outcomes.effect_size_raw: no difference between virtual reality and non-virtual reality games
outcomes.source_quote: We observed no difference between preparatory and distracting games, or between virtual reality and non-virtual reality games.

studies.study_label: Comparison of first-person shooter gamers (FPSG) and non-players (NP) on sound-induced flash illusion and migraine incidence

population.population_raw: a group of expert first-person shooter gamers and a non-players group

studies.context: We tested this hypothesis by comparing the temporal profile of the sound-induced illusion in a group of expert first-person shooter gamers and a non-players group.

interventions.intervention_name: Group membership: exposure to first-person shooter videogames

outcomes.outcome_raw: windows of illusion (~87 ms) relative to non-players (~105 ms)
outcomes.measurement_raw: temporal window of illusion measured in milliseconds (TBW)
outcomes.source_quote: gamers experience signiﬁcantly narrower windows of illusion (~87 ms) relative to non-players (~105 ms)

outcomes.outcome_raw: higher veridical reports in gamers (~68%) relative to non-players (~59%)
outcomes.measurement_raw: percentage of veridical reports during SIFI task
outcomes.source_quote: leading to higher veridical reports in gamers (~68%) relative to non-players (~59%)

outcomes.outcome_raw: migraine severity may be directly proportioned to the time spent on videogames
outcomes.outcome_canonical: migraine incidence/severity related to videogame exposure
outcomes.measurement_raw: not_reported (relationship described qualitatively)
outcomes.source_quote: we tested whether audio-visual intensive training in gamers could be related to the incidence of migraine, and found that its severity may be directly proportioned to the time spent on videogames.

studies.study_label: Case study of Chilean game developers and agencies (semi-structured interviews)

population.population_raw: Chilean game developers and governmental agencies

studies.context: Chilean video games sector, peripheral location; case study deploying semi-structured interviews with Chilean game developers and governmental agencies; discussion framed by dependency theory and cognitive capitalism.

outcomes.outcome_raw: forms of dependency on the use of non-proprietary technological tools (game engines and software development kits – SDKs) and the protection of intellectual property (IP) in the centre (publishing schemes)
outcomes.measurement_raw: semi-structured interviews
outcomes.source_quote: Deploying semi-structured interviews with Chilean game developers and governmental agencies, the article evidence forms of dependency on the use of non-proprietary technological tools (game engines and software development kits – SDKs) and the protection of intellectual property (IP) in the centre (publishing schemes).

outcomes.outcome_raw: use of non-proprietary software limits creativity and imposes barriers for dissemination of information among small actors; game development is conditioned by technological limits of SDKs and game engines
outcomes.outcome_canonical: constraints from non-proprietary tools (game engines/SDKs) on creativity, dissemination, and cooperation
outcomes.measurement_raw: semi-structured interviews; literature citations (Kirkpatrick, Dyer-Witheford, O'Donnell)
outcomes.source_quote: Tools and software used for creating games are not proprietary software of small developers. Instead, these developers usually use software which is available for free, or on a licence basis, to create and edit games. The use of non-proprietary software has led to critiques from games scholars, specifically that the use of said tools (Game Engines and Software Development Kits for consoles) not only limits creativity (Kirkpatrick, 2013) but also imposes essential barriers for the dissemination of information among small actors.

outcomes.outcome_raw: the primary source of value in cognitive capitalism and global game production (IP) is eventually transferred to publishers in exchange for financial stability, or games developers are transformed into service providers of outsourced content under publishers' schemes and IP trade
outcomes.outcome_canonical: transfer of IP value to publishers and transformation of developers into service providers
outcomes.measurement_raw: semi-structured interviews; case study analysis
outcomes.source_quote: The empirical data show that the primary source of value in cognitive capitalism and global game production (IP) is eventually transferred to publishers in exchange for financial stability, or games developers are transformed into service providers of outsourced content (partial or full development of games) under publishers’ schemes and IP trade.
